meta.title: Who makes, feeds, runs, challenges and enforces the UK tax system
coverage: The principal central, devolved and local tax authorities and their different roles.
Representative data, filing, account, payment, compliance, debt and challenge paths.
Professional, software, payment, debt-recovery and insolvency permission boundaries.
Named current private collaborators only where an official source supports the relationship.
Selected decided cases that expose how the machinery works in practice.
exclusions: No taxpayer records, identifiers, balances, credentials or case files.
No private or residential contacts, named junior staff, allegations or inferred influence network.
No claim that this is every tax, council, contract, software supplier or enforcement route.
No prediction of how a court, tribunal, regulator or authority will decide a live matter.
editorialRules: Law, judgment, policy, guidance, manuals and registers keep different authority labels.
Registration, professional authorisation, AML supervision, client authority, system access and software recognition are never treated as synonyms.
A named framework supplier is not called an HMRC contractor without an HMRC-specific award or statement.
Conditional enforcement choices are a graph, not an inevitable ladder.
Every substantive record names evidence fields and a source locator; gaps and source conflicts stay visible.
meta.warning: A researched public map, not legal advice or a live authority record. Check the cited law and current official guidance for an actual matter.

sources.title: Taxation and spending
supports: The Commons authorises taxation and the parliamentary process turns financial resolutions and bills into law.
doesNotProve: That Parliament administers an individual tax account or decides a taxpayer's liability.

sources.title: Tax policy making principles
supports: The government's stated framework for predictable, coherent and evidence-based tax policy making.
doesNotProve: That every tax measure follows the framework perfectly or that policy guidance is binding law.

sources.title: Commissioners for Revenue and Customs Act 2005
supports: HMRC's statutory Commissioners, their collection and management functions, appointment of officers and Charter duty.
doesNotProve: The elements, due date or enforcement route for any particular tax.

sources.title: HM Revenue & Customs: About us
supports: HMRC's functions and the policy partnership in which HM Treasury leads strategic tax policy while HMRC leads implementation and maintenance.
doesNotProve: That HMRC sets tax rates without Parliament or that it collects every local or devolved tax.

sources.title: HMRC governance
supports: HMRC is a non-ministerial department led by Commissioners in whom CRCA functions are vested.
doesNotProve: The identity of the officer assigned to a taxpayer or the legality of a specific decision.

sources.title: The HMRC Charter
supports: HMRC's published service standards, taxpayer expectations and statutory Charter framework.
doesNotProve: A separate right of appeal where tax legislation provides none or an automatic stay of collection.

sources.title: Valuation Office joins HM Revenue and Customs
supports: The VOA ceased to be an executive agency on 1 April 2026 and the Valuation Office now performs property valuation inside HMRC.
doesNotProve: That local authorities stopped issuing and collecting Council Tax or business-rates bills.

sources.title: Introduction to business rates
supports: The Valuation Office values non-domestic property while local councils send bills and collect business rates.
doesNotProve: A property's rateable value, relief eligibility or account balance.

sources.title: Council Tax arrears
supports: The England and Wales reminder, full-year demand, liability-order and possible local-enforcement path.
doesNotProve: That every council has taken each step or that the same procedure applies in Scotland or Northern Ireland.

sources.title: Land & Property Services
supports: LPS combines property valuation, rates billing and collection, land registration and mapping functions in Northern Ireland.
doesNotProve: That Great Britain's local-authority model applies to Northern Ireland.

sources.title: About Revenue Scotland
supports: Revenue Scotland's non-ministerial status, accountability and devolved taxes administered.
doesNotProve: That Revenue Scotland administers UK-wide Income Tax, VAT or Corporation Tax.

sources.title: Welsh Revenue Authority: About us
supports: WRA's collection and management role for devolved Welsh taxes and its stated operating approach.
doesNotProve: That WRA administers Council Tax or every Welsh tax receipt.

sources.title: Border Force: About us
supports: Border Force performs physical customs and border interventions and protects customs revenue.
doesNotProve: That Border Force administers ordinary domestic tax accounts or replaces HMRC customs policy and systems.

sources.title: PAYE5025: Real Time Information submission types
supports: Employers submit an FPS when paying employees; reported pay and deductions update HMRC liability and individual records.
doesNotProve: That a manual is primary law or that every payroll record is accurate.

sources.title: Bank and building society interest returns
supports: UK banks and building societies report interest annually; HMRC uses it for account pre-population, coding and return checks.
doesNotProve: That all bank transactions or account balances are routinely reported under this return.

sources.title: Collect and verify digital platform seller information
supports: Reporting platform operators collect, verify and report prescribed seller, transaction and payment information to HMRC.
doesNotProve: That every platform user owes tax or that gross reported receipts equal taxable profit.

sources.title: Sign in to HMRC online services
supports: Government Gateway and GOV.UK One Login coexist across HMRC services, with different account routes for individuals, businesses and agents.
doesNotProve: That a sign-in credential is client authority or that One Login has replaced Gateway for every service.

sources.title: Apply for an agent services account
supports: ASA registration requirements, staff permissions and HMRC's express statement that it does not endorse agents.
doesNotProve: Professional competence, a reserved-legal-services licence or client authority for a specific taxpayer.

sources.title: Check if and when you need to register as a tax adviser with HMRC
supports: The phased 2026 registration duty for paid advisers who interact with HMRC and the consequences of not registering.
doesNotProve: That all UK tax advice is a reserved or licensed profession or that registration is endorsement.

sources.title: HMRC Developer Hub terms of use
supports: Production-access review, security and service conditions, revocability and the permitted phrase 'HMRC recognised'.
doesNotProve: HMRC approval, accreditation, endorsement or a legal relationship with a developer.

sources.title: SDLT recognition instructions
supports: The test, vendor identifier and recognition process for third-party SDLT return software.
doesNotProve: General legal accuracy, security accreditation or approval of every product feature.

sources.title: Government Banking Service
supports: Government Banking's public-sector service and named financial partners Barclays, Citibank, NatWest, Bottomline and Worldpay.
doesNotProve: A complete contract graph, spend-to-date, service levels or incident history for each partner.

sources.title: Open Banking Services award CW95696
supports: HMRC awarded Ecospend a £2,260,457 Open Banking service contract running 29 August 2024 to 28 August 2027.
doesNotProve: Spend-to-date, service uptime or access to customers' online-banking credentials.

sources.title: Digital Platforms Run and Change award CW93522
supports: HMRC awarded Accenture UK a contract valued up to £305.69 million for live digital platforms and application support from 20 May 2024 to 19 May 2029.
doesNotProve: Spend-to-date, sole ownership of a named HMRC system or responsibility for any incident.

sources.title: Income Tax Sub Domain award CW98653
supports: HMRC awarded Capgemini UK a £100 million Income Tax Sub Domain contract from 15 January 2025 to 14 January 2031.
doesNotProve: Spend-to-date, every system component, subcontractor or service-level result.

sources.title: HMRC annual report 2024 to 2025: accountability
supports: Aggregate annual accountability, technology-contract scale and programme risks disclosed by HMRC.
doesNotProve: A complete current supplier-to-system topology or unredacted contract performance.

sources.title: Self Assessment Legal Framework: the SA account
supports: Self Assessment uses a single charge account with statements, payments on account and balancing payments.
doesNotProve: That the published account view exposes every internal ledger movement or that an assessment is always required before payment becomes due.

sources.title: DMBM210160: Self Assessment payment allocation
supports: Published ordering for allocating Self Assessment payments and handling excess or unallocated amounts.
doesNotProve: The allocation of a particular payment or a unified cross-tax ledger.

sources.title: What will happen if you do not pay your tax bill
supports: HMRC's support-first position, current named private debt agencies and conditional civil enforcement options across UK jurisdictions.
doesNotProve: A fixed order of enforcement or the current England and Wales taking-control fees, which conflict with newer regulations and HMRC's manual.

sources.title: DMBM800040: Time to Pay policy
supports: HMRC's published policy for affordable Time to Pay arrangements based on circumstances and ability to pay.
doesNotProve: An entitlement to a particular instalment term or that interest stops.

sources.title: DMBM605420: checks before enforcement action
supports: Published pre-enforcement quality checks include whether debt is overdue, unpaid and normally not disputed or appealed.
doesNotProve: A freestanding statutory defence or that an internal check was performed correctly in a particular case.

sources.title: DMBM657020: taking control of goods
supports: HMRC's current operational summary of the England and Wales taking-control power, notices, agent status and £79 compliance fee.
doesNotProve: That the older public non-payment fee table is current or that a manual overrides legislation.

sources.title: Taking Control of Goods (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2026 explanatory memorandum
supports: The 1 May 2026 fee and minimum-notice changes accompanying the amended taking-control regulations.
doesNotProve: That every older GOV.UK summary was updated or that the rules apply outside England and Wales.

sources.title: Direct Recovery of Debts: your rights and responsibilities
supports: The £5,000 protected balance, hold notice, objection grounds and County Court appeal route for direct bank-account recovery.
doesNotProve: That every overdue debt qualifies or that a bank independently decides the tax liability.

sources.title: How insolvency practitioners are authorised in Great Britain
supports: Recognised professional bodies authorise insolvency practitioners and the Insolvency Service oversees those bodies for the Secretary of State.
doesNotProve: That the oversight body can reverse an office-holder's case decision; generally only a court can do that.

sources.title: Certificated Enforcement Agent Register
supports: County Court judges issue two-year certificates for private enforcement agents using the taking-control procedure.
doesNotProve: That HMRC officers need certification; legislation exempts them, or that a certificate authorises tax assessment.

sources.title: Disagree with a tax decision or penalty: get a review
supports: Statutory review is performed by an HMRC officer not previously involved and has separate time limits before tribunal.
doesNotProve: That every HMRC communication is appealable or that review automatically postpones payment.

sources.title: Complain about HMRC
supports: HMRC's two-tier service complaint route and later escalation to the Adjudicator and PHSO through an MP.
doesNotProve: That a complaint replaces a tax appeal, pauses appeal time or suspends payment.

sources.title: How to appeal to the First-tier Tribunal (Tax Chamber)
supports: The independent Tax Chamber's scope, procedure and public contact details.
doesNotProve: That every dispute falls within the Tribunal's jurisdiction or that every decision is published.

sources.title: The role of the Adjudicator
supports: The Adjudicator reviews unresolved service complaints but cannot replace statutory review or appeal.
doesNotProve: Power to rewrite tax law, determine liability or hear a tribunal appeal.

sources.title: About the National Audit Office
supports: The NAO audits public bodies and examines value for money for Parliament.
doesNotProve: Power to decide an individual tax assessment or complaint.

sources.title: Public Accounts Committee: role
supports: PAC scrutinises economy, efficiency and effectiveness in public spending and service delivery.
doesNotProve: Power to determine individual tax liability or resolve a private complaint.

sources.title: Treasury Committee: role
supports: The Committee examines the expenditure, administration and policy of HM Treasury, HMRC and associated bodies.
doesNotProve: Day-to-day operational control of HMRC or a right to intervene in an individual case.

sources.title: HMRC criminal investigation powers and safeguards
supports: Only trained authorised officers in FIS and RIS may use HMRC criminal powers, with civil and criminal powers used for distinct purposes.
doesNotProve: That ordinary inability to pay is a crime or that HMRC itself decides whether to prosecute.

sources.title: HMRC criminal investigation policy
supports: Independent prosecutors decide charges: CPS in England and Wales, COPFS in Scotland and PPSNI in Northern Ireland.
doesNotProve: That an investigation will lead to charge or conviction.

sources.title: Anti-money laundering registration
supports: Regulated businesses must be supervised by the applicable HMRC or professional-body AML supervisor.
doesNotProve: Professional competence, HMRC endorsement or authority to perform reserved legal work.

sources.title: Office for Professional Body Anti-Money Laundering Supervision
supports: OPBAS currently oversees professional-body AML supervisors, not their individual member firms.
doesNotProve: That the FCA has already replaced all current tax, accountancy and legal AML supervisors.

sources.title: Approved regulators
supports: The Legal Services Board oversees approved legal regulators, including regulators for solicitors and licensed conveyancers.
doesNotProve: That all tax advice is reserved legal work or that every tax adviser is a lawyer.

sources.title: CIOT professional standards
supports: CIOT standards bind members and connect disciplinary matters to the Taxation Disciplinary Board.
doesNotProve: Statutory regulation of every person offering UK tax advice.

sources.title: HMRC non-payment guidance: debt collection agencies
supports: HMRC states its named debt agencies are FCA-regulated, may use letter, SMS and phone, and never visit.
doesNotProve: A live allocation to a named agency, contract value or coercive enforcement powers.

sources.title: HMRC v Tooth
supports: The decided appeal, citation and issues concerning discovery assessments, staleness and deliberate inaccuracy.
doesNotProve: That every inaccurate return is deliberate or that assessment time limits are collection limitation periods.

sources.title: Bartram v HMRC
supports: No ordinary FTT appeal lies against a TMA section 28C determination; the taxpayer's appeal was dismissed.
doesNotProve: That a determination can never be superseded by a timely return or that Special Relief is unavailable.

sources.title: Perrin v HMRC
supports: Reasonable excuse is objective but assessed in the actual taxpayer's circumstances; the taxpayer's appeal was dismissed.
doesNotProve: That genuine belief alone is always reasonable or that HMRC need not establish the underlying failure.

sources.title: BPP Holdings v HMRC
supports: The Supreme Court upheld the tribunal's ability to enforce procedural rules and bar HMRC from resisting an appeal after serious non-compliance.
doesNotProve: That every procedural breach produces a debarring order.

sources.title: R (Haworth) v HMRC
supports: HMRC's appeal failed and follower and accelerated-payment notices remained quashed because the statutory threshold was not met.
doesNotProve: That all follower notices or accelerated-payment notices are unlawful.

sources.title: Hurstwood Properties v Rossendale Borough Council
supports: The councils' appeal succeeded in litigation concerning business-rates avoidance schemes using special-purpose vehicles.
doesNotProve: That company separateness can generally be ignored or that all tax planning is ineffective.

sources.title: Totel Ltd v HMRC
supports: The VAT statutory pay-first rule with hardship relief survived the legal challenge; the taxpayer's appeal failed.
doesNotProve: That direct-tax appeal routes use the same payment rule or that hardship relief is automatic.

sources.title: R v Allen
sources.publisher: UK Parliament: House of Lords judgments
supports: A criminal tax-fraud conviction, sentence and confiscation order following dishonest concealment and false returns.
doesNotProve: That simple inability or even civil refusal to pay is itself criminal tax fraud.

sources.title: Taxes Management Act 1970
supports: Core Income Tax Self Assessment notification, return, enquiry, assessment, payment-postponement and recovery machinery.
doesNotProve: The rules for every other tax or the facts of an individual liability.

sources.title: Finance Act 2007 Schedule 24
supports: Penalty framework for inaccuracies in documents and failures to notify under specified conditions.
doesNotProve: That an error was careless or deliberate in a particular case.

sources.title: Finance Act 2008
supports: Schedule 36 information powers and statutory collection powers including taking control, Scottish summary warrants and set-off.
doesNotProve: That every power is available in every jurisdiction or without statutory preconditions.

sources.title: Finance Act 2009
supports: Schedules 53, 55 and 56 provide interest, late-filing and late-payment frameworks for specified taxes.
doesNotProve: A timeless rate or identical penalty model for every obligation.

sources.title: Tribunals, Courts and Enforcement Act 2007
supports: The tribunal structure and Schedule 12 taking-control-of-goods machinery for England and Wales.
doesNotProve: Northern Ireland distraint or Scottish diligence rules.

sources.title: Finance (No. 2) Act 2015 Schedule 8
supports: Direct recovery of tax debts through bank and building-society information, holds, objections and transfer notices.
doesNotProve: That a bank assesses tax or that HMRC may ignore the statutory safeguards.

sources.title: Insolvency Act 1986
supports: The statutory framework for bankruptcy, company winding up and insolvency-practitioner authorisation.
doesNotProve: That insolvency is automatic after tax non-payment or that HMRC directs an appointed office-holder.

sources.title: Finance Act 2026
supports: The statutory basis for mandatory registration of paid tax advisers who interact with HMRC for clients.
doesNotProve: A general statutory licence for every form of tax advice.

sources.title: Money Laundering Regulations 2017
supports: AML customer-due-diligence, record, reporting and supervision duties for regulated businesses.
doesNotProve: That AML supervision is the same as tax-adviser registration or professional authorisation.

sources.title: Legal Services Act 2007
supports: The England and Wales approved-regulator and reserved-legal-activity framework.
doesNotProve: That tax advice as such is always a reserved legal activity.

sources.title: Local Government Finance Act 1988
supports: The non-domestic rating framework, valuation lists and local billing structure.
doesNotProve: A specific property's liability or the law in Northern Ireland.

sources.title: Tax Collection and Management (Wales) Act 2016
supports: WRA's devolved collection, enquiry, assessment, information, penalty, review and appeal machinery.
doesNotProve: HMRC's UK-wide processes or Scottish devolved-tax law.

sources.title: Revenue Scotland and Tax Powers Act 2014
supports: Revenue Scotland's statutory constitution and devolved tax administration, investigation, penalty, review and appeal framework.
doesNotProve: HMRC's central-tax process or Welsh devolved-tax law.

sources.title: DMBM519215: PAYE RTI submissions and charges
supports: Commercial software or Basic PAYE Tools sends FPS data to RTI Core, which updates ETMP employer charges and EBS employee deductions.
doesNotProve: A complete technical architecture, security topology or current status of every legacy acronym.

sources.title: DMBM210155: payment accounting systems
supports: HMRC manuals name multiple accounting and payment systems and distinguish allocated, transferred and unmatched money.
doesNotProve: One unified taxpayer ledger, real-time consistency or public access to internal case data.

actors.name: Taxpayers and liable persons
roles: Keep records and notify, report, self-assess or pay where the applicable law requires it.
Authorise agents or software; challenge appealable decisions through the correct route.
actors.explanation: The system is not entirely authority-calculated. In Self Assessment the liable person supplies facts and computes a liability, while employers and other collectors may withhold or report in other regimes.
actors.whyItExists: Tax law places the primary obligation on the person whose income, transaction, property, employment or other fact creates the charge.

evidence.locator: Sections 7 to 9: notice, returns and self-assessment

transparencyNotes: This is a legal role, not a public list of people. The dataset contains no taxpayer identity or account information.

actors.name: UK Parliament, with financial primacy in the House of Commons
roles: Debate and enact primary tax law.
Authorise taxation and scrutinise delegated powers and administration.
actors.explanation: Government proposes most tax measures, but a charge needs legal authority made through Parliament; HMRC administers rather than invents the charge.
actors.whyItExists: Taxation requires democratic and legal authorisation, with the elected Commons holding financial primacy.
transparencyNotes: This actor does not include devolved legislatures, which can make law only within devolved competence.

actors.name: HM Treasury
roles: Lead strategic tax-policy development and economic policy.
Sponsor Budget and Finance Bill measures with ministers.
actors.explanation: The Treasury chooses and frames government tax policy; Parliament makes the law and HMRC leads implementation and maintenance.
actors.whyItExists: Tax choices must be joined to fiscal, economic and spending policy rather than made case by case by the collecting authority.
transparencyNotes: The published principles are a policy framework, not a cause of action or guarantee that every measure follows the ideal process.

actors.name: HM Revenue & Customs and its Commissioners
roles: Collect and manage revenues assigned by law.
Administer central taxes, customs, National Insurance and specified payments and benefits.
Appoint officers, operate accounts and systems, check compliance, issue decisions and collect debt within statutory powers.
actors.explanation: CRCA vests functions in Commissioners; trained officers exercise delegated powers. HMRC is a non-ministerial department, not the legislature or the court hearing appeals from it.
actors.whyItExists: A permanent revenue administration applies Parliament's rules, receives information and money, manages compliance and accounts to government and Parliament.
transparencyNotes: No single public register joins every officer delegation, agent authority, software production credential, debt allocation and contract dependency.

actors.name: Valuation Office inside HMRC
roles: Maintain Council Tax bands and non-domestic rating valuations.
Handle valuation checks and challenges and perform statutory rent-officer work.
actors.explanation: Since 1 April 2026 the work sits inside HMRC. It values property; local billing authorities generally calculate and collect the resulting local tax bills.
actors.whyItExists: A specialist national valuation function separates the assessment of property value from councils' billing and collection interests.

evidence.locator: Announcement, paragraphs on 1 April 2026 integration and continuing services

transparencyNotes: Many official URLs and pages still say 'VOA'; that is now an alias for the Valuation Office function, not evidence that the executive agency still exists.

actors.name: Local billing authorities
roles: Issue Council Tax and non-domestic-rates bills, apply local reliefs and collect amounts due.
Seek liability orders or use the territory's local-debt processes where bills remain unpaid.
actors.explanation: This is a class of councils, not one national collector. Account systems, policies, contacts, contractors and complaint data are decentralised.
actors.whyItExists: Property taxes finance local services and are administered by local billing bodies using statutory valuation inputs.

evidence.locator: Overview and how the bill is calculated

transparencyNotes: The API deliberately does not pretend that one national local-tax account, policy or enforcement interface exists.

actors.name: Land & Property Services Northern Ireland
roles: Value property, issue and collect rates bills, maintain land registers and deliver mapping services.
actors.explanation: Northern Ireland combines valuation and rates collection in LPS instead of using Great Britain's council-plus-valuation-office model.
actors.whyItExists: Northern Ireland has its own devolved property, rating and land-administration structure.
transparencyNotes: Valuation, billing and title registration remain distinct legal functions even when housed in one organisation.

actors.name: Revenue Scotland
roles: Administer Land and Buildings Transaction Tax, Scottish Landfill Tax and Scottish Aggregates Tax.
Operate devolved return, payment, compliance, penalty, review and appeal processes.
actors.explanation: Revenue Scotland is a non-ministerial department accountable to the Scottish Parliament; HMRC still administers reserved and assigned UK taxes unless law says otherwise.
actors.whyItExists: Scottish devolution assigns specified tax powers to Scottish institutions while leaving other taxes in the UK system.
transparencyNotes: Its agent-authority status is not exposed through a broad public machine API.

actors.name: Welsh Revenue Authority
roles: Collect and manage Land Transaction Tax and Landfill Disposals Tax and prepare additional devolved services.
Use statutory enquiry, assessment, information, inspection, penalty, review and appeal machinery.
actors.explanation: WRA administers taxes devolved to Wales; HMRC and local authorities continue to administer other taxes in Wales.
actors.whyItExists: Welsh devolution assigns specified tax powers to a Welsh non-ministerial authority accountable through Welsh institutions.
transparencyNotes: Online account and agent processes are documented, but no general public authority-status API was identified.

actors.name: Border Force
roles: Carry out physical customs controls, searches, seizures and border interventions under the HMRC partnership.
actors.explanation: HMRC remains the customs policy, system and revenue authority while Border Force performs front-line border interventions.
actors.whyItExists: Physical border enforcement requires trained operational officers integrated with immigration and security functions.
transparencyNotes: The principal published HMRC partnership agreement is old; operational boundaries should not be inferred beyond published material.

actors.name: Department for Work and Pensions
roles: Receive relevant PAYE RTI information from HMRC for Universal Credit and other authorised purposes.
Participate in the shared National Insurance identity environment.
actors.explanation: DWP is a downstream public-data user in the payroll flow, not HMRC's ordinary tax-debt collector.
actors.whyItExists: Real-time earnings information allows means-tested benefits to respond to reported pay without asking claimants to duplicate every payroll fact.

evidence.locator: RTI submission and downstream record use

transparencyNotes: Published privacy and manual material does not form one field-level, system-level catalogue of all HMRC-DWP sharing.

actors.name: Employers, pension payers, banks, building societies and reporting platforms
roles: Report payroll when payments are made, annual interest, or prescribed digital-platform seller data under separate rules.
Collect or verify fields required by the applicable reporting regime.
actors.explanation: These organisations supply facts to HMRC. They do not decide the final liability merely because they report income, interest, deductions or receipts.
actors.whyItExists: Third-party reporting reduces information gaps, supports pre-population and lets HMRC compare a return with independently supplied records.

evidence.locator: Information to collect, verify and report

transparencyNotes: The class groups different statutory regimes for navigation; it does not imply one shared dataset, legal basis or reporting frequency.

actors.name: Government Digital Service
roles: Operate GOV.UK One Login identity and sign-in services used by migrating government services.
actors.explanation: One Login authenticates users for participating services; HMRC still operates tax accounts, authorisations and many Government Gateway journeys.
actors.whyItExists: A reusable cross-government identity layer reduces separate sign-in systems while leaving service-specific authority with each department.

evidence.locator: GOV.UK One Login and Government Gateway sign-in routes

transparencyNotes: One Login is not yet a universal HMRC credential and does not itself confer authority to act for a client.

actors.name: Government Banking Service
roles: Provide banking, payment and cash-management services to public-sector bodies.
Procure and manage financial-sector partners for government payment rails.
actors.explanation: Government Banking is administered by HMRC but serves many public bodies; it moves and reconciles money rather than deciding the underlying tax charge.
actors.whyItExists: Central procurement and banking expertise gives public bodies common secure access to commercial payment networks.
transparencyNotes: The public page does not join each partner to current contract, value, service-level and incident records.

actors.name: Named Government Banking financial partners
roles: Barclays, Citibank, NatWest, Bottomline and Worldpay provide named banking or payment services to Government Banking.
actors.explanation: They supply payment infrastructure under separate commercial arrangements; the public list is not a statement that every partner handles every tax payment.
actors.whyItExists: Government uses regulated commercial networks for bank transfers, cards, international payments and related services.
transparencyNotes: No contract value, allocation, performance or universal current scope is inferred from being named on the page.

actors.name: Ecospend Technologies Limited
roles: Provide HMRC-contracted Payment Initiation and Account Information Services for payment and repayment journeys.
actors.explanation: The official award proves a time-bounded contract and ceiling, not access to users' bank sign-in secrets or responsibility for tax decisions.
actors.whyItExists: Open Banking lets a payer authorise a bank payment directly without manually re-keying government bank details.
transparencyNotes: The £2,260,457 figure is the published total contract value, not spend-to-date.

actors.name: Accenture (UK) Limited
roles: Supply HMRC live digital-platform run, change, application-support and mobile-app services under award CW93522.
actors.explanation: The award establishes a current supplier relationship and maximum value; it does not establish sole ownership of every platform or blame for service outcomes.
actors.whyItExists: HMRC procures external technical capacity to run and change large digital-service estates alongside civil-service teams.
transparencyNotes: £305.69 million is the published total/maximum contract value, not spend-to-date or a service-quality score.

actors.name: Capgemini UK plc
roles: Supply design, development, operation and live support in HMRC's Income Tax Sub Domain under award CW98653.
actors.explanation: The award establishes a current supplier relationship and £100 million total value; redacted schedules leave component ownership and detailed performance incomplete.
actors.whyItExists: HMRC procures multidisciplinary delivery teams and domain expertise for major income-tax services.
transparencyNotes: The published value does not expose spend-to-date, subcontractors, exact systems or service-level results.

actors.name: HMRC-named private debt collection agencies
roles: LCS, Advantis, Ardent/DRS, Bluestone, BPO, BWL, CCS Collect, Moorcroft and Pastdue may contact assigned debtors by letter, SMS or phone.
Take payment or discuss Time to Pay and return unresolved cases to HMRC.
actors.explanation: These firms are contact and payment-discussion providers. HMRC says they never visit; they do not assess tax, list goods or exercise HMRC officer powers.
actors.whyItExists: Private contact capacity expands HMRC's ability to reach debtors while coercive decisions remain with statutory actors.

contacts.label: Current official agency list and complaint route

transparencyNotes: The current public list does not disclose live allocation, fee, recovery outcome, vulnerability handling or complaint metrics per firm.

actors.name: Financial Conduct Authority and OPBAS
roles: Regulate applicable consumer-credit debt-collection activity.
Through OPBAS, oversee professional-body AML supervisors rather than their member firms directly.
actors.explanation: FCA regulation of a DCA and OPBAS oversight of a professional body are different functions. The planned future single AML-supervisor model is not yet the current universal state.
actors.whyItExists: Independent conduct regulation and regulator-of-supervisors oversight address risks created when private bodies handle finance, debt and AML supervision.

evidence.locator: HMRC statement that named agencies are FCA regulated

transparencyNotes: A future FCA professional-services AML role must be modelled as announced until transition law and commencement are in force.

actors.name: First-tier Tribunal, Tax Chamber
jurisdictions: United Kingdom, subject to statutory jurisdiction
roles: Hear most statutory tax and penalty appeals from HMRC and specified other authorities.
Make independent findings and case-management orders.
actors.explanation: The Tribunal hears appealable decisions. It is not HMRC's service-complaint office and does not gain jurisdiction merely because a taxpayer disagrees.
actors.whyItExists: Independent adjudication separates the authority that made the decision from the body deciding a legal challenge.

evidence.locator: What the Tribunal can hear and contact details

transparencyNotes: Not every FTT decision is published and tribunal appeal is separate from service complaint.

actors.name: Upper Tribunal and appellate courts
jurisdictions: United Kingdom, with territory-specific appellate routes
roles: Hear permitted appeals on legal error from the Tax Chamber and subsequent appellate courts.
Give binding or persuasive judgments according to court hierarchy.
actors.explanation: The exact route depends on jurisdiction and permission. This grouped node describes the appellate function, not one court or universal leap to the Supreme Court.
actors.whyItExists: Appeal courts correct legal error and develop consistent interpretation beyond the first-instance tribunal.
transparencyNotes: Official judgment coverage and appeal-status metadata are incomplete across UK courts and publications.

actors.name: County courts, sheriff courts and Northern Ireland civil courts
roles: Grant or review jurisdiction-specific debt orders, summary warrants, charging remedies, insolvency orders and direct-recovery objections where law assigns the decision to a court.
actors.explanation: Courts authorise judicial remedies; HMRC may apply as creditor but does not grant its own court order. Some statutory HMRC powers do not require a prior order.
actors.whyItExists: Judicial control supplies an independent decision point before specified coercive consequences or on statutory objections.

evidence.locator: County court, Scottish summary warrant and insolvency sections

transparencyNotes: One grouped node must not be used to transplant England and Wales remedies into Scotland or Northern Ireland.

actors.name: Adjudicator's Office
roles: Review eligible unresolved HMRC service complaints after HMRC's internal complaint stages.
actors.explanation: The Adjudicator examines administration, fairness and use of discretion; it cannot substitute for statutory appeal or decide the tax charge.
actors.whyItExists: An impartial external office-holder provides a later review of maladministration after the department has had two chances to resolve it.
transparencyNotes: The office is presented as impartial but organisationally linked to HMRC; the Adjudicator is an external office-holder.

actors.name: Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman
roles: Consider eligible unresolved central-government service complaints referred through an MP after earlier complaint routes.
actors.explanation: PHSO is at the end of the service-complaint ladder, not the tax-appeal hierarchy.
actors.whyItExists: Parliamentary ombudsman review supplies independent investigation of central-government maladministration outside the tax merits appeal.
transparencyNotes: The MP referral filter and complaint eligibility are separate from statutory tax appeal deadlines.

actors.name: National Audit Office
roles: Audit HMRC accounts and examine economy, efficiency and effectiveness for Parliament.
Publish value-for-money findings and recommendation tracking.
actors.explanation: The NAO scrutinises systems and spending, not individual liabilities or service complaints.
actors.whyItExists: Parliament needs independent evidence about whether revenue administration uses public money properly and performs effectively.
transparencyNotes: Audit criticism is not a finding that a particular tax assessment is invalid.

actors.name: House of Commons Public Accounts Committee
roles: Scrutinise whether public money was used with economy, efficiency and effectiveness, including delivery through private providers.
actors.explanation: PAC examines administration and spending after the event; it does not set the tax or resolve an individual case.
actors.whyItExists: Elected parliamentary scrutiny follows public money through departments and contractors using NAO evidence.
transparencyNotes: Committee reports are scrutiny findings and recommendations, not court judgments.

actors.name: House of Commons Treasury Committee
roles: Examine HM Treasury, HMRC and associated bodies' expenditure, administration and policy.
actors.explanation: The Committee scrutinises policy and administration and can call for people, papers and records; it does not operate HMRC.
actors.whyItExists: Departmental select-committee scrutiny makes ministers and senior officials answer publicly for policy and administration.
transparencyNotes: Committee scrutiny does not suspend collection or create an individual appeal route.

actors.name: HMRC Fraud Investigation Service and Risk and Intelligence Service
roles: Trained authorised officers investigate serious suspected tax crime using jurisdiction-specific criminal powers.
Use civil powers only for civil assessment, collection or penalties, not as a shortcut to prove a criminal charge.
actors.explanation: Not every officer in FIS or RIS is authorised, and ordinary compliance or Debt Management staff do not acquire criminal powers from handling a case.
actors.whyItExists: Serious dishonest attacks on the tax system require trained investigators and evidence safeguards comparable to other law enforcement.

evidence.locator: Who can use criminal powers and division of civil and criminal powers

transparencyNotes: Operational selection criteria and some internal guidance are withheld or only partly public for law-enforcement reasons.

actors.name: Independent UK prosecuting authorities
roles: CPS, COPFS or PPSNI independently decide whether evidence and public interest justify criminal charges in their jurisdiction.
Present criminal tax cases in court.
actors.explanation: HMRC investigates tax crime; the relevant independent prosecutor decides whether to prosecute. A civil debt case does not become criminal merely because it remains unpaid.
actors.whyItExists: Separating investigation from charging decisions supplies an independent evidential and public-interest check.

contacts.label: CPS contact and other jurisdiction links

transparencyNotes: This grouped navigation node does not merge the distinct legal systems, codes or contacts of the three prosecutors.

actors.name: Tax agents and advisers
roles: Advise clients, prepare documents and interact with HMRC when authorised and permitted.
Maintain separate professional, AML, HMRC-registration, account and client-authority requirements where each applies.
actors.explanation: UK tax advice is not one universally licensed profession. From 2026 paid interaction with HMRC is subject to phased registration, but that is not endorsement and does not replace other regulation.
actors.whyItExists: Complex tax law creates demand for representation and software-assisted compliance, while safeguards protect both client confidentiality and system integrity.

evidence.locator: Who needs to register and exceptions

transparencyNotes: There is no cited unified public register joining ASA status, AML supervision, professional membership, legal authorisation, discipline and client authority.

actors.name: HMRC and professional-body AML supervisors
roles: Register or supervise in-scope accountancy, tax and legal service providers under the Money Laundering Regulations.
Monitor compliance and use supervisory sanctions within their legal remit.
actors.explanation: HMRC supervises providers not covered by an eligible professional body; bodies such as CIOT, ATT, ICAEW and legal regulators supervise qualifying members or firms. OPBAS oversees professional bodies, not firms directly.
actors.whyItExists: Tax, accountancy and legal services can be used to move or disguise criminal property, so the regulations require risk-based controls and supervision.
transparencyNotes: The government's future FCA model is a transition plan, not proof that current supervisors have ceased.

actors.name: Legal Services Board and approved legal regulators
roles: LSB oversees approved regulators; SRA regulates solicitors and firms; CLC regulates licensed conveyancers and other approved regulators cover their professions.
actors.explanation: Legal authorisation governs lawyers and reserved legal work. It is separate from HMRC agent registration, AML supervision and general tax advice.
actors.whyItExists: Reserved legal activities and client-money risks need profession-specific entry, conduct, complaints and discipline controls.
transparencyNotes: Scotland and Northern Ireland have separate legal-profession structures not expanded in this v1 actor node.

actors.name: Tax and accountancy professional bodies and disciplinary schemes
roles: Award qualifications and set member conduct, tax-practice, insurance and continuing-development rules.
Use schemes such as the Taxation Disciplinary Board for CIOT and ATT member discipline.
actors.explanation: Professional standards bind members and supervised firms; they are not statutory control over every unmembered tax adviser.
actors.whyItExists: Voluntary and chartered professions create competence, ethics and discipline frameworks beyond minimum state registration.
transparencyNotes: Membership or an AML register entry is not HMRC endorsement and cannot be extrapolated to non-members.

actors.name: Certificated or exempt enforcement agents
roles: Use the Schedule 12 taking-control procedure when a valid enforcement power and notice exist.
Private agents normally hold County Court certification; HMRC officers acting in this capacity are exempt from certification.
actors.explanation: Certification permits the taking-control role. It does not authorise tax assessment, make every visit lawful or turn a debt collection caller into an enforcement agent.
actors.whyItExists: Taking control of physical goods is coercive, so private actors face fitness, certification and procedure controls.

evidence.locator: Register purpose, issuing court and complaints

transparencyNotes: HMRC's current private DCA list is a different role and those firms are expressly said not to visit.

actors.name: Licensed insolvency practitioners
jurisdictions: United Kingdom, with territory-specific insolvency law
roles: Act as trustees, liquidators, administrators or supervisors when law and appointment require a licensed office-holder.
Realise assets and distribute proceeds according to statutory duties rather than HMRC instructions.
actors.explanation: HMRC may petition or vote as creditor, but it does not control an appointed insolvency practitioner; the office-holder owes statutory duties and courts control legal challenges to actions.
actors.whyItExists: Independent licensed office-holders manage collective creditor processes and protect the estate rather than letting one creditor seize everything first.

evidence.locator: Who may act and complaint/court boundaries

transparencyNotes: The exact office, appointing route and territorial law must be checked; 'insolvency practitioner' is not a generic debt collector.

actors.name: Recognised professional bodies for insolvency
roles: ICAEW, ICAS and the Insolvency Practitioners Association authorise, monitor and discipline eligible insolvency practitioners.
actors.explanation: The Secretary of State recognises the bodies; they license practitioners, while the Insolvency Service oversees the bodies.
actors.whyItExists: Specialist professional regulators assess competence and fitness and monitor office-holder conduct under a statutory recognition framework.
transparencyNotes: An RPB handles professional-conduct complaints but generally cannot reverse an office-holder's case decision; that is for a court.

actors.name: Insolvency Service
roles: Oversee recognised professional bodies on behalf of the Secretary of State.
Operate public insolvency functions and enforcement within its statutory remit.
actors.explanation: It is the regulator of insolvency regulators, not the day-to-day controller of every licensed practitioner's appointment.
actors.whyItExists: State oversight holds private professional authorisers to statutory regulatory objectives.
transparencyNotes: Only a court can generally confirm, reverse or modify an insolvency practitioner's action in an appointment.

actors.name: Tax software and API providers
jurisdictions: United Kingdom and overseas providers serving UK users
roles: Build software that calculates, keeps records or submits through HMRC interfaces.
Meet production-access, OAuth, security, privacy and tax-specific recognition conditions where applicable.
actors.explanation: Production API access, a user OAuth grant and tax-specific software recognition are separate. 'HMRC recognised' is permitted wording; 'approved' and 'accredited' are not.
actors.whyItExists: Third-party software turns legal and technical rules into usable workflows and is now a major filing channel.

evidence.locator: Production access, marketing, security and authorisation

transparencyNotes: Public supplier lists do not expose tested version, security-review date, uptime, incident history, ownership changes or removal reasons.

actors.name: Scottish Parliament and Senedd Cymru
roles: Enact primary devolved tax legislation within their separate constitutional competences.
Scrutinise devolved tax authorities and ministers through territory-specific procedures.
actors.explanation: This grouped navigation node keeps devolved law-making separate from Westminster; it does not merge the two legislatures or their competences.
actors.whyItExists: Devolution assigns specified tax law-making and accountability to elected institutions in Scotland and Wales.

contacts.label: Scottish Parliament and Senedd public sites

evidence.locator: Act of the Scottish Parliament metadata
Act of the National Assembly for Wales metadata

transparencyNotes: Northern Ireland's devolved legislature and tax-law competence are not expanded in this v1 node.

relationships.explanation: Parliament enacts the legal powers and charges that HMRC administers; the direction does not mean Parliament handles individual accounts.
HM Treasury leads strategic tax-policy development while HMRC leads implementation and maintenance in the published policy partnership.
HMRC administers central tax obligations and accounts for liable persons within powers conferred by tax law.
The Valuation Office has operated inside HMRC since 1 April 2026; its statutory valuation services continue.
Valuation lists and bands supply the valuation input from which local billing authorities calculate property-tax bills.
Local billing authorities issue and collect Council Tax and non-domestic-rates bills; the relationship is decentralised council by council.
LPS values, bills and collects domestic and non-domestic rates in Northern Ireland under its separate system.
Revenue Scotland administers the taxes devolved to it, not the whole UK tax code.
WRA administers devolved Welsh taxes within its statutory powers, alongside other HMRC and local tax systems in Wales.
Separate legal regimes require payroll, interest and platform information to flow from private data holders to HMRC for account, calculation or compliance uses.
Relevant PAYE RTI data supports DWP Universal Credit calculation; this does not make DWP HMRC's ordinary debt collector.

evidence.locator: Use of RTI deductions and records

relationships.explanation: HMRC administers Government Banking as a service for government and wider public-sector bodies.
Government Banking publicly names Barclays, Citibank, NatWest, Bottomline and Worldpay as financial partners; no universal scope or value is inferred.
Award CW95696 supplies Open Banking payment and repayment services for the published 2024 to 2027 term.
Award CW93522 covers live digital-platform run and change services for the published 2024 to 2029 term.
Award CW98653 covers HMRC Income Tax Sub Domain delivery and support for the published 2025 to 2031 term.
HMRC currently names these agencies as firms it may use for contact, payment and Time to Pay discussion; the page does not prove allocation or contract value per firm.

evidence.locator: Current agency list and permitted contact

relationships.explanation: HMRC states all agencies on its current list are regulated by the FCA; that regulation does not grant tax-enforcement powers.
The independent Tax Chamber hears statutory appeals against appealable HMRC decisions within its jurisdiction.
The Tax Chamber can hear statutory appeals from WRA decisions assigned to it by Welsh tax legislation.
With permission and on legal-error grounds, the Upper Tribunal begins the appellate route from the Tax Chamber.
County Court judges certify private enforcement agents for two years; HMRC officers acting as enforcement agents are statutorily exempt.
Eligible service complaints can move from HMRC's two internal stages to the Adjudicator; this is not an appeal on liability.
An unresolved eligible central-government complaint may proceed after the Adjudicator to PHSO through an MP.
NAO audits HMRC accounts and examines value for money for Parliament without deciding individual tax cases.
PAC scrutinises HMRC's economy, efficiency and effectiveness, often using NAO evidence, but cannot redetermine a tax assessment.
The Treasury Committee scrutinises HMRC expenditure, administration and policy; it does not run operations.
Authorised HMRC officers investigate and build evidence; the relevant independent prosecuting authority makes the charging decision.
A client authority permits the agent to act within its scope; neither an ASA nor professional membership creates this authority by itself.

evidence.locator: Request client authorisation and staff permissions

relationships.explanation: HMRC registers in-scope paid advisers for interaction and can deny that interaction or sanction non-compliance; registration is expressly not endorsement.
The applicable HMRC or professional-body supervisor monitors in-scope firms under the Money Laundering Regulations; scope depends on business and membership facts.
OPBAS oversees professional bodies only in their AML-supervision capacity, not every membership, qualification or disciplinary function and not firms directly.
Legal regulators control the subset of advisers who are regulated lawyers or perform reserved legal work, not the whole tax-advice market.
Professional bodies impose standards and discipline only on members, students and supervised firms within their scheme.
Recognised professional bodies license and monitor eligible insolvency practitioners under the statutory recognition framework.

evidence.locator: Insolvency practitioners and recognised professional bodies

relationships.explanation: The Insolvency Service oversees recognised professional bodies on behalf of the Secretary of State; it does not normally rehear the merits of an office-holder's act.

evidence.locator: Insolvency Service oversight and complaint boundary

relationships.explanation: HMRC grants production API access and tax-specific recognition subject to separate tests and terms; it says recognised, not approved or accredited.

frameworks.name: Law, policy and administration are separate layers
frameworks.publicRationale: Tax should be legally authorised and democratically scrutinised, while a permanent expert administration applies the law consistently.
frameworks.howItWorks: Ministers and HM Treasury develop policy; Parliament enacts charges and powers; HMRC, devolved authorities and councils administer only the regimes assigned to them; courts decide legal challenges.
frameworks.tension: Operational guidance can feel like law to a taxpayer even when it is only HMRC's interpretation, and broad collection-and-management powers still need tax-specific legal limits.

frameworks.name: Self Assessment: declare first, check later
frameworks.publicRationale: People with the relevant facts calculate and report their position by a deadline instead of waiting for the authority to inspect everyone first.
frameworks.howItWorks: The taxpayer files and self-assesses; HMRC may enquire, correct, determine where no return is made or make a discovery assessment within the applicable rules and time limits.
frameworks.tension: The label can hide asymmetric knowledge and power: taxpayers must understand complex law, while a no-return determination can become enforceable without an ordinary appeal and needs a timely statutory remedy.

frameworks.name: Withholding and third-party reporting
frameworks.publicRationale: Collect close to the payment event and compare independent records so tax is less dependent on one annual declaration.
frameworks.howItWorks: Employers report and withhold through PAYE RTI; banks report interest; platform operators collect and report seller and transaction information under separate statutory regimes.
frameworks.tension: Reported gross receipts are not automatically taxable profit, matching can be wrong, and public documentation does not expose one complete field-level data-flow catalogue.

evidence.locator: Full Payment Submission and record updates
Purpose and scope of interest returns

frameworks.name: Identity, access and client authority are separate
frameworks.publicRationale: Digital services need to know who signed in, which organisation they belong to and whether they may act for someone else without sharing the taxpayer's password.
frameworks.howItWorks: Government Gateway or One Login authenticates; ASA identifies and permissions an agent organisation; the client separately authorises the agent; an API application separately receives production credentials and a user grant.
frameworks.tension: Coexisting identity systems and private production-access decisions are hard to verify externally, and users can mistake account possession for legal authority.

evidence.locator: Client authority, staff permissions and no endorsement

frameworks.name: A debt needs a charge, an account and an allocation
frameworks.publicRationale: Money should be pursued only when a legally due amount has been placed on the correct person's account and payments and credits have been applied properly.
frameworks.howItWorks: A return, determination or assessment creates or confirms a charge; HMRC systems allocate payments by published rules; Debt Management receives work items only after due dates and account processing.
frameworks.tension: Published manuals reveal multiple ledgers and manual reallocations rather than one transparent unified account, so a payment can exist without appearing where a debtor expects.

frameworks.name: Support first, then a conditional enforcement graph
frameworks.publicRationale: People who cannot pay should reach an affordable path; people who can pay but do not engage may face proportionate recovery so public revenue is not optional.
frameworks.howItWorks: Contact, Time to Pay, set-off or coding may resolve debt. If not, facts and jurisdiction choose among private contact, goods, bank recovery, court orders, Scottish diligence, Northern Ireland processes, insolvency or international assistance.
frameworks.tension: HMRC says powers are not in a fixed order. Public summaries can lag binding changes, and a complaint, appeal, accounting write-off and legal extinction of debt are four different things.

frameworks.name: Tax appeal and service complaint are different doors
frameworks.publicRationale: The merits of a legal tax decision and the quality or fairness of administration need different remedies and decision-makers.
frameworks.howItWorks: An appeal or statutory review challenges an appealable decision; the FTT is independent. A complaint runs through HMRC's two tiers, then the Adjudicator and potentially PHSO through an MP.
frameworks.tension: A complaint does not preserve an appeal deadline or automatically stop payment, while some payment-during-appeal rules differ sharply between direct and indirect taxes.

frameworks.name: Civil collection is not criminal prosecution
frameworks.publicRationale: Most errors and unpaid tax are assessed, penalised and recovered civilly; serious suspected dishonesty may require criminal investigation and independent prosecution safeguards.
frameworks.howItWorks: Ordinary compliance and Debt Management use civil powers. Only trained authorised FIS or RIS officers use criminal powers, and CPS, COPFS or PPSNI independently decides whether to prosecute.
frameworks.tension: Civil and criminal processes can run at the same time for different lawful purposes, so the power used—not just the team name—must be recorded.

evidence.locator: Authorised officers and division of powers

frameworks.name: There is no single UK collector for every tax
frameworks.publicRationale: Devolution and local finance assign different taxes and administrative powers to institutions closer to the relevant territory or service.
frameworks.howItWorks: HMRC administers central taxes; local authorities and LPS administer property rates through different models; Revenue Scotland and WRA administer specified devolved taxes.
frameworks.tension: A UK-wide interface can easily erase territorial law, different courts, different account systems and different agent-authority rules.

rules.title: Commissioners for Revenue and Customs Act 2005
rules.explanation: Creates the Commissioners and HMRC functions, including collection and management, officer appointment, confidentiality and the Charter duty.

rules.title: Taxes Management Act 1970
rules.explanation: Provides core Income Tax Self Assessment notification, return, enquiry, determination, discovery, postponement, appeal and recovery machinery.

rules.title: Finance Act 2007 Schedule 24 inaccuracy penalties
rules.explanation: Links specified document inaccuracies to behaviour, potential lost revenue, reductions, assessment and appeal rules.

rules.title: Finance Act 2008 Schedule 36 information and inspection powers
rules.explanation: Permits HMRC to require information and documents or conduct specified inspections subject to statutory purposes, approvals, restrictions, penalties and appeal rules.

rules.title: Finance Act 2009 Schedules 53, 55 and 56
rules.explanation: Provides statutory frameworks for interest, late-filing penalties and late-payment penalties for specified obligations; rates and tax coverage remain effective-dated.

rules.title: Finance Act 2008 section 127 and TCEA 2007 Schedule 12 taking control of goods
rules.explanation: Gives HMRC an enforcement power in England and Wales and supplies the notice, entry, control, valuation and sale procedure, as amended by regulations.

rules.title: Finance (No. 2) Act 2015 Schedule 8 direct recovery of debts
rules.explanation: Permits qualifying tax debt to be recovered through bank and building-society account holds and transfers, with protected balances, objection and court safeguards.

rules.title: Insolvency Act 1986
rules.explanation: Provides collective company and individual insolvency processes, court orders, office-holder duties and practitioner authorisation.

rules.title: Finance Act 2026 mandatory tax-adviser registration
rules.explanation: Requires in-scope businesses paid to interact with HMRC about clients' tax affairs to register through the phased 2026 to 2027 process, subject to exceptions.

rules.title: Money Laundering Regulations 2017
rules.explanation: Require risk assessment, due diligence, records, reporting, controls and supervision for in-scope tax, accountancy, legal and other businesses.

rules.title: Legal Services Act 2007
rules.explanation: Creates the England and Wales oversight and approved-regulator framework and defines reserved legal activities; tax advice alone is not made universally reserved.

rules.title: Local Government Finance Act 1988 non-domestic rating framework
rules.explanation: Creates the valuation-list and billing framework for non-domestic rates in England and Wales, with local authorities collecting from ratepayers.

rules.title: Tax Collection and Management (Wales) Act 2016
rules.explanation: Creates common WRA administration, return, enquiry, assessment, information, penalty, review and appeal machinery for devolved Welsh taxes.

rules.title: Revenue Scotland and Tax Powers Act 2014
rules.explanation: Creates Revenue Scotland and common devolved administration, investigation, penalty, review and appeal machinery in Scotland.

rules.title: HMRC Charter
rules.explanation: Publishes behavioural standards for HMRC and customers under a CRCA statutory duty; it does not create every remedy or override tax legislation.

accountTypes.name: Government Gateway sign-in
accountTypes.holder: Individual, organisation or agent user depending on the service
accountTypes.purpose: Authenticate a user into many HMRC and customs services and bind service enrolments to that digital identity.
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: Credentials prove control of the sign-in. They do not by themselves prove professional status or authority to act for a client.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Enrolment can expose tax-service views or actions available to that user or organisation; tax charges remain in separate tax accounts.
notTheSameAs: UTR, NINO, VAT number or EORI

evidence.locator: Business Gateway ID, staff permissions and client authorisation

transparencyNotes: HMRC does not publish one machine-readable service-by-service migration and credential crosswalk.

accountTypes.name: GOV.UK One Login
accountTypes.holder: Individual user of a migrated government service
accountTypes.purpose: Provide a reusable cross-government sign-in and identity-checking route for participating services.
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: Authentication grants entry only to participating services; the destination service still decides the user's permissions.
accountTypes.accountEffects: For migrated HMRC journeys it opens the personal service, while many business, agent, customs and software journeys still require Gateway.

evidence.locator: GOV.UK One Login sign-in route and coexistence

transparencyNotes: Migration is partial as at review date; do not treat One Login as a universal HMRC credential.

accountTypes.name: Personal Tax Account
accountTypes.purpose: Expose selected personal tax records, services, messages and actions through one user-facing view.
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: The person signs in through the current HMRC identity route and can use only services made available to that identity.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Shows or changes supported records; it is a presentation and service surface, not proof that all HMRC ledgers are one database.
transparencyNotes: The public view is not a field-level description of underlying NPS, Self Assessment and other internal systems.

accountTypes.name: Business Tax Account
accountTypes.holder: Business or organisation and its permissioned team
accountTypes.purpose: Bring supported business taxes and HMRC services into a shared online view.
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: Organisation users and team permissions control access; a complete view may depend on one correctly enrolled business identity.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Displays supported liabilities, returns, payments or messages but does not erase tax-specific account rules.
transparencyNotes: A combined user interface does not establish a single underlying tax accounting system.

accountTypes.name: Agent Services Account
accountTypes.holder: Registered tax-adviser legal entity with permissioned staff
accountTypes.purpose: Register an in-scope adviser business and give it access to specified modern HMRC agent services.
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: The firm must meet registration conditions; each staff member uses a controlled identity and clients grant separate authority.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Allows supported interactions for authorised clients. It can be restricted or unavailable if registration conditions are not met.

evidence.locator: Application, staff permissions, authorisation and no endorsement

transparencyNotes: HMRC exposes no cited public ASA search endpoint for a client or API consumer to verify status.

accountTypes.name: HMRC Developer Hub application and OAuth grants
accountTypes.holder: Software-provider organisation and separately authorising users
accountTypes.purpose: Identify an application, grant sandbox or production credentials and represent a user's scoped consent without collecting their HMRC password.
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: HMRC reviews production access; the user separately completes OAuth for user-restricted endpoints. Access is revocable.
accountTypes.accountEffects: API calls read or change only the resources and scopes granted; application access alone cannot act for a taxpayer.

evidence.locator: Production access, handling data and customer authorisation

transparencyNotes: Production review responses and per-application access status are private; no public application register was identified.

accountTypes.name: Self Assessment single charge account
accountTypes.holder: Person or entity within Self Assessment
accountTypes.purpose: Record Self Assessment charges, credits, interest, penalties and payment allocations and produce statements.
identifiers: payment reference formed under current HMRC instructions
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: The account is created and maintained under tax administration rules; the identifier directs records or money but is not an authentication secret.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Payments normally allocate by statutory due date, year and charge-creation order; excess can remain unallocated or be reallocated.
notTheSameAs: proof that a displayed amount cannot be disputed
transparencyNotes: A payment can be received yet not be allocated to the expected charge; the public interface does not expose every internal ledger event.

accountTypes.name: Tax-specific account and payment references
accountTypes.holder: Taxpayer, employer, trader or organisation in the relevant regime
accountTypes.purpose: Route returns, charges and payments to VAT, PAYE, Corporation Tax, customs or other tax-specific records.
identifiers: PAYE reference and Accounts Office reference
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: References identify a record or payment destination; authentication and client authority are separate controls.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Using the wrong reference can delay or misallocate money and may require manual correction even where funds reached HMRC.
notTheSameAs: permission to act for the record holder

evidence.locator: Employer, employee and deduction identifiers in FPS

transparencyNotes: The list names identifier schemes only; this corpus never stores an actual taxpayer identifier or value.

accountTypes.name: Local and devolved tax accounts
accountTypes.holder: Property taxpayer or devolved-tax filer in the relevant territory
accountTypes.purpose: Hold or present bills, returns, payments, reliefs, agent authority and case status under separate local or devolved regimes.
identifiers: WRA online tax account and transaction references
accountTypes.accessAndAuthority: Each operator sets its own identity, enrolment and agent-authority process under its law and service design.
accountTypes.accountEffects: Actions affect only the relevant local or devolved regime; no national UK account aggregates them all.
transparencyNotes: No common national API exposes every council balance, recovery state, relief decision or enforcement assignment.

systems.name: Government Gateway
systems.purpose: Authenticate individuals, organisations and agents for HMRC services and bind service enrolments.
transparencyNotes: Authentication is not client authority and the public migration map to One Login remains incomplete.

systems.name: GOV.UK One Login
systems.purpose: Provide cross-government sign-in and identity verification for participating services, including migrating HMRC individual journeys.
inputs: Sign-in and identity-check evidence supplied by the user
outputs: Authenticated identity assertion to the participating service

evidence.locator: One Login route and service boundaries

transparencyNotes: Existing customers, businesses, agents, customs and commercial-software paths may still use Gateway.

systems.name: Personal Tax Account and Business Tax Account service layer
systems.purpose: Present supported tax records and actions through human-facing online accounts.
inputs: data drawn from tax-specific back ends
outputs: supported filings, changes and payment journeys

evidence.locator: Digital Platforms Run and Change scope

transparencyNotes: The awards establish broad current service relationships, not a definitive component-by-component supplier topology for these portals.

systems.name: Agent Services Account service
systems.purpose: Register eligible adviser businesses, manage staff access and connect client authorisations to modern HMRC agent services.
transparencyNotes: No public verification endpoint exposes an adviser's ASA status.

systems.name: HMRC Developer Hub and APIs
systems.purpose: Publish API specifications, issue application credentials and mediate authorised machine-to-machine tax-service calls.
transparencyNotes: Application approvals and access scopes are private; recognition lists reveal little assurance evidence.

systems.name: PAYE RTI submission and processing chain
systems.purpose: Receive employer Full Payment Submissions and update employer charges and employee deduction records.
inputs: FPS and EPS data from payroll software or HMRC tools
outputs: records used for tax and authorised benefit purposes

evidence.locator: RTI Core, ETMP and EBS update path

transparencyNotes: Manual acronyms describe operational data flow, not a complete or security-sensitive architecture.

systems.name: HMRC tax accounting and record systems
systems.purpose: Record tax-specific charges, credits, payments, interest and identifiers across systems such as ETMP, NPS, EBS/BROCS, CESA, SAFE and OAS.
transparencyNotes: The grouping is a documented system family, not a claim that one unified ledger or one supplier operates all named components.

systems.name: Integrated Debt Management System and Debt Management casework
systems.purpose: Receive overdue work items, segment risk, drive contact and record debt-case actions and outcomes.
inputs: Due and unpaid charges from tax accounts
appeal, vulnerability and Time to Pay information
outputs: referrals to support, private contact or enforcement branches
transparencyNotes: Public manuals expose rules and legacy names but not a real-time queue, risk model, allocation or complete audit trail.

systems.name: Government Banking and payment rails
systems.purpose: Receive, send and reconcile government money through bank transfer, card and other payment networks.
outputs: reconciliation data passed to the tax account
transparencyNotes: Payment clearing and tax-ledger allocation are separate events; partner pages and awards are not one joined operational record.

systems.name: HMRC Open Banking payment service
systems.purpose: Initiate payer-authorised bank payments and support account-information uses defined by the HMRC contract.
inputs: customer authorisation completed with their bank
transparencyNotes: The contract proves provider, scope, value and term; it does not publish live transaction data, uptime or incident history.

systems.name: Local and devolved tax filing, billing and account systems
systems.purpose: Operate territory- and authority-specific property-tax bills, devolved returns, payments, reliefs, agent permissions and casework.
outputs: devolved assessments, statements, penalties, reviews and appeal records
transparencyNotes: This grouping is navigational: there is no shared UK schema, account API or common authority-status endpoint.

permissions.name: Client authority to act
permissions.requiredFor: An agent or software service acting for a taxpayer within the specified tax and action scope.
holderTypes: Tax agent
legal representative
software application using a user grant
permissions.enforcement: HMRC can refuse disclosure or action outside a valid authority and the client can revoke authority; misuse may engage professional, civil, data-protection or criminal consequences.

permissions.name: Mandatory HMRC tax-adviser registration
permissions.requiredFor: In-scope paid business interaction with HMRC about a client's tax affairs after the applicable staged registration window.
holderTypes: UK or overseas legal entity providing paid tax assistance and interacting with HMRC, subject to exceptions
permissions.enforcement: An adviser required to register may be unable to interact with HMRC and may face sanctions if it continues attempting to do so.

evidence.locator: Scope, dates, exceptions and non-registration consequences

permissions.name: Agent Services Account access
permissions.requiredFor: Use of specified HMRC agent services and the 2026 adviser-registration channel.
holderTypes: Registered adviser legal entity
permissioned staff user
permissions.enforcement: HMRC controls account registration, service access and staff permissions and can restrict access where conditions are not met.

permissions.name: Anti-money-laundering supervision
permissions.requiredFor: Carrying on an in-scope regulated tax, accountancy, legal or trust/company service business without another applicable exemption.
holderTypes: Firm supervised by HMRC
eligible member or firm supervised by a professional body
regulated legal practice
permissions.enforcement: Supervisors may refuse or cancel registration, inspect, direct remediation and use civil or disciplinary sanctions; criminal offences remain for specified breaches.

evidence.locator: OPBAS oversees professional bodies, not firms

permissions.name: Legal-profession authorisation
permissions.requiredFor: Practising as the applicable regulated legal professional and performing reserved legal activities within the authorised scope.
holderTypes: Solicitor or authorised firm
licensed conveyancer
other authorised legal professional
permissions.enforcement: The profession's regulator can investigate, impose permitted sanctions or refer serious matters to the relevant disciplinary tribunal.

permissions.name: Tax professional membership and qualification
permissions.requiredFor: Using protected professional designations or remaining a member in good standing under the body's rules.
holderTypes: CIOT, ATT or other professional-body member, student or firm within the body's scheme
permissions.enforcement: The body or its disciplinary scheme may censure, fine, suspend or remove members under its rules.
notEquivalentTo: statutory regulation of every tax adviser

permissions.name: HMRC API production access
permissions.requiredFor: Calling an HMRC API in the production environment as an identified software application.
holderTypes: Developer application operated by a software provider
permissions.enforcement: HMRC reviews applications against its terms and may remove or revoke Developer Hub and API access temporarily or permanently.

evidence.locator: Production access review, revocation and marketing

permissions.name: HMRC API user OAuth grant
permissions.requiredFor: A software application calling a user-restricted API within the scopes the user authorises.
holderTypes: Developer application acting for an authenticated taxpayer or agent user
permissions.enforcement: Scopes and token lifetime limit calls; the user or HMRC can end access and the developer must protect and delete tokens under the terms.

permissions.name: HMRC tax-software recognition
permissions.requiredFor: Being listed as recognised for the particular HMRC filing or tax service and using any assigned vendor identifier.
holderTypes: Tax software product and provider for the named service
permissions.enforcement: HMRC controls testing, listing and continued interface access and may remove recognition or access where requirements fail.

permissions.name: Certificated enforcement-agent status
permissions.requiredFor: A private individual using the England and Wales taking-control-of-goods procedure unless another statutory exemption applies.
holderTypes: Fit and proper private enforcement agent
HMRC officer as an exempt rather than certificated agent
permissions.enforcement: A County Court judge issues the certificate for two years, considers renewal and can receive serious complaints about fitness.
