notEquivalentTo: court order for a particular debt

evidence.locator: HMRC officer exemption and enforcement-power prerequisite

permissions.name: Insolvency-practitioner authorisation
permissions.requiredFor: Acting as a statutory insolvency office-holder such as trustee, liquidator, administrator or arrangement supervisor.
holderTypes: Qualified individual authorised by a recognised professional body
permissions.enforcement: The authorising body monitors and disciplines the practitioner; the Insolvency Service oversees bodies and courts control challenges to appointment actions.
notEquivalentTo: authority to change a tax assessment

evidence.locator: Who may act, RPBs, oversight and complaints

permissions.name: FCA-regulated debt-collection activity
permissions.requiredFor: Applicable regulated consumer-credit debt-collection activity by an HMRC-named private agency.
holderTypes: Private debt collection agency
permissions.enforcement: FCA conduct rules and complaints processes apply; HMRC also says it reviews agencies against its own process and guidance.
notEquivalentTo: proof of a live HMRC debt allocation

evidence.locator: FCA regulation, permitted contact, no visits and HMRC review

pipelineStages.name: Policy proposal and legal authority
pipelineStages.trigger: Government identifies a policy change or existing law already creates the obligation.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HM Treasury and ministers develop policy; Parliament enacts primary law and may authorise delegated detail; HMRC prepares administration and guidance.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: No tax charge or coercive power should arise merely because an administrator wants it; legal authority and accountable policy come first.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: None by itself until the enacted rule and triggering facts create an obligation or authorise an administrative act.
rightsAndSafeguards: Parliamentary scrutiny and public law limits
Point-in-time law must be matched to the transaction or period

pipelineStages.name: Register the person, organisation, agent or application
pipelineStages.trigger: A taxable activity, filing duty, employer role, business enrolment or authorised digital service needs a stable record.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HMRC creates or links tax-specific records and identity enrolments; a user authenticates through the supported sign-in; agents, applications and client grants follow separate registration paths.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Returns, payments and powers must attach to the correct legal person and authorised channel.
outputs: ASA or developer application where applicable
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Creates or links account surfaces but does not itself create a tax charge or client authority.
rightsAndSafeguards: Tax identifiers are not passwords
Staff and client permissions remain separable
HMRC registration is not endorsement

pipelineStages.name: Collect facts from third parties
pipelineStages.trigger: An employer pays staff, a bank credits reportable interest, a platform has reportable sellers or another statutory data event occurs.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The data holder collects prescribed fields and submits them to HMRC; HMRC matches them to records and may use them for charges, pre-population, coding, checks or authorised sharing.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Independent, timely data reduces under-reporting and repetitive fact collection and lets withholding happen near payment.
inputs: seller identity, transactions and payment details
pipelineStages.accountEffects: May create PAYE employer charges or update records; a reported gross receipt does not automatically settle the recipient's final tax liability.
rightsAndSafeguards: Each reporting regime has its own scope and correction route
A taxpayer can challenge an assessment built from wrong matching
Data minimisation and confidentiality duties still apply

evidence.locator: FPS timing, content and record effects
RTI Core to ETMP and EBS

pipelineStages.name: Return, self-assessment, determination or authority assessment
pipelineStages.trigger: A filing notice or statutory obligation matures, or HMRC has legal grounds to assess or determine without a return.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The taxpayer submits facts and, where required, a self-assessment. HMRC processes it and may correct, enquire, determine or later make a discovery assessment under the applicable law.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: The return turns facts into a declared legal position; authority assessments prevent non-filing from making liability unenforceable.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Creates, replaces or changes a charge on the applicable tax account and starts tax-specific payment, enquiry and appeal clocks.
rightsAndSafeguards: Assessment and enquiry time limits are tax- and fact-specific
Appeal rights depend on the statutory decision
A section 28C determination has no ordinary appeal but may be superseded only through the statutory return route or exceptional relief

pipelineStages.name: Create the charge and allocate credits or payments
pipelineStages.trigger: A return, assessment, payroll submission, penalty, interest event or payment produces an account entry.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: Tax-specific systems post charges and credits. Payments are matched by references and allocated under account rules; unmatched or excess funds may need transfer or manual reallocation.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Enforcement and statements require a quantified, due amount after recognised credits and payments are applied to the correct person and charge.
outputs: statement balance and possible debt work item
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Self Assessment usually allocates by earliest statutory due date, earliest year and charge creation order; other regimes use their own account rules.
rightsAndSafeguards: A debtor can raise missing-payment or allocation evidence
Account presentation does not remove statutory appeal rights
Manuals describe practice but legislation controls liability

pipelineStages.name: Receive, clear and reconcile payment
pipelineStages.trigger: The taxpayer, agent, employer, bank or debt agency initiates a payment or HMRC issues a repayment.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: Commercial payment rails settle money to government; reference and settlement data is reconciled to the tax ledger; a DCA remits cleared payment to HMRC.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: A legal liability is not discharged merely by intending to pay; cleared funds must reach government and be credited to the right account.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: A correct allocation reduces the charge; an unmatched receipt can leave the displayed debt open until corrected.
rightsAndSafeguards: Customers authenticate Open Banking with their bank
A private DCA payment is credited to HMRC after clearing
Keep payment evidence and challenge misallocation promptly

evidence.locator: Payment Initiation and Account Information service award

pipelineStages.name: Compliance check, information request and decision
pipelineStages.trigger: Risk selection, a return enquiry window, mismatch, repayment claim, missing information or suspected error justifies an intervention under the applicable law.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: An authorised officer may open an enquiry, request relevant information, inspect where permitted and issue a closure, assessment, amendment or penalty decision.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Self-reporting and third-party data still need verification so the authority can correct underpayment and protect compliant taxpayers from unequal treatment.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: A valid decision may add or reduce a charge and starts the applicable review, appeal and payment rules.
rightsAndSafeguards: Information powers need a statutory purpose and respect restrictions and privileges
Appealable decisions must state the route and deadline
Civil powers cannot be misused to gather proof for a criminal charge

pipelineStages.name: Accrue interest and assess late or inaccuracy penalties
pipelineStages.trigger: A relevant amount is unpaid after its statutory date, a required filing is late or a qualifying inaccuracy or failure is established.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: Interest accrues under the applicable regime and HMRC assesses statutory penalties using the tax, date, behaviour and duration rules.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Interest compensates for late use of public money; penalties encourage timely and accurate compliance and distinguish conduct.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Adds separate charges to the account; Time to Pay commonly does not stop interest unless law says otherwise.
rightsAndSafeguards: Penalty and reasonable-excuse rights are statutory and fact-specific
HMRC must establish the penalty-triggering failure
Rates and models are effective-dated and cannot be treated as timeless constants

pipelineStages.name: Internal review, tribunal appeal and payment question
pipelineStages.trigger: The taxpayer disagrees with a decision that legislation makes reviewable or appealable and acts within the applicable deadline.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: A different HMRC officer may conduct a statutory review; the taxpayer may notify the FTT; permitted legal-error appeals can move upward with permission.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: A decision-maker should be reviewable by someone not involved and ultimately by an independent judicial body.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Dispute does not always suspend payment. Direct-tax postponement and indirect-tax pay-first or hardship rules must be checked separately; interest may continue.
rightsAndSafeguards: Typical appeal clocks are short and a service complaint does not preserve them
Review should be by an officer not previously involved
FTT is independent and has its own jurisdiction and procedure

evidence.locator: Continue paying and separate complaint route

pipelineStages.name: Debt reminder, contact, vulnerability and Time to Pay
pipelineStages.trigger: A valid account charge reaches its due date unpaid or a planned payment fails.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HMRC contacts the debtor, checks circumstances and can agree affordable Time to Pay, use credits, code qualifying debt or resolve a dispute before stronger action.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Early engagement collects more with less harm and distinguishes temporary inability from deliberate non-engagement.
outputs: referral to private contact or pre-enforcement
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Debt remains on the account and interest may continue; a kept arrangement changes the pursuit path, not necessarily the legal due date.
rightsAndSafeguards: HMRC says it normally tries contact before action except suspected fraud or crime
Extra support and vulnerability should shape contact
A debtor can dispute amount or payment rather than silently ignore contact

evidence.locator: Get help and contact before action

pipelineStages.name: Private debt-agency contact
pipelineStages.trigger: HMRC assigns a case to one of the agencies on its current public list for contact and payment discussion.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The agency may contact by letter, SMS or phone, take payment or discuss Time to Pay, remit cleared funds and return unresolved cases to HMRC.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: External contact capacity helps HMRC reach more debtors without delegating tax decisions or coercive officer powers.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: The agency does not own the tax debt; cleared payment is credited to the HMRC account.
rightsAndSafeguards: The agency never visits
It must use HMRC process and FCA-compliant complaints handling
It cannot list goods, assess tax or exercise HMRC enforcement powers

evidence.locator: Agency payment and return to HMRC

pipelineStages.name: Validate the debt and choose an enforcement branch
pipelineStages.trigger: Contact and voluntary resolution fail or the case returns from a private agency, and HMRC considers coercive recovery.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HMRC checks that the debt is due, correctly linked and actionable, considers dispute, appeal, address, means and vulnerability, then selects a lawful jurisdiction-specific option.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Coercion should not start against the wrong person, an unripe charge, a resolved dispute or without considering proportionate alternatives.
inputs: contact history, means, assets and vulnerability
outputs: court, Scottish, Northern Irish, insolvency or international referral
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Debt, interest and permitted enforcement fees can remain or increase; the chosen branch does not redetermine the tax merits.
rightsAndSafeguards: Published Quality Gateway checks
Each power has separate notices, thresholds and objections
HMRC says enforcement options are not listed in a fixed order

evidence.locator: When powers are used and no fixed order

pipelineStages.name: Take control of goods or use the territorial equivalent
pipelineStages.trigger: A due debt, valid statutory enforcement power and required notice support goods action in the relevant jurisdiction.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: In England and Wales an agent may take control, value and ultimately sell non-exempt goods under Schedule 12; Scotland and Northern Ireland use different procedures and officers.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Saleable property can satisfy an enforceable debt where voluntary payment fails, subject to notice, time, exempt-goods and sale controls.
inputs: goods ownership, value and exemption facts
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Current England and Wales cases from 1 May 2026 can add £79 compliance, £247 enforcement and £116 sale fees plus percentages above the current threshold; territorial rules differ.
rightsAndSafeguards: Current minimum notice is 14 clear days, or 28 after a qualifying debt-advice request for relevant non-business debt
Essential and exempt goods are protected
Valuation and sale notices apply
Private certification and HMRC officer exemption are different statuses

pipelineStages.name: Direct Recovery of Debts from bank accounts
pipelineStages.trigger: A qualifying established debt above the statutory threshold remains after required contact and non-engagement safeguards, with sufficient protected funds available.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HMRC obtains account information, directs a hold up to the debt, gives the debtor the hold notice and objection window, then may direct transfer if no successful objection or resolution occurs.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Liquid funds can satisfy established debt without goods sale or insolvency while preserving a minimum balance and an objection route.
outputs: No action, hold, released hold or transferred funds
pipelineStages.accountEffects: At least £5,000 must remain available across accounts; transferred money credits the tax debt, and joint-account shares and third-party interests have specific treatment.
rightsAndSafeguards: Thirty calendar days to object on listed grounds
HMRC decides a timely objection within 30 working days
A rejected objection can go to County Court
Vulnerability and exceptional-hardship safeguards apply

evidence.locator: Schedule 8 hold, objection and payment machinery
Protected balance, objection and County Court appeal

pipelineStages.name: Court remedies, territorial diligence, international recovery or insolvency
pipelineStages.trigger: A final enforceable debt remains and facts justify a court order, Scottish summary warrant, Northern Ireland process, foreign assistance or insolvency as a last course.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HMRC may seek charging, earnings or third-party orders, use territorial diligence, request foreign recovery or petition for bankruptcy or winding up. Courts grant judicial orders and an appointed office-holder controls an insolvency estate.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Some debts require judicial control, access to property or a collective process where many creditors and future viability must be considered.
inputs: Final debt and evidence of service
assets, earnings, property or foreign connection
outputs: write-off or remission where recovery is impractical
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Recoveries credit debt; interest, costs and priority depend on law. Accounting write-off does not always extinguish the legal liability.
rightsAndSafeguards: The court, not HMRC, grants the relevant judicial order
Insolvency is described as final-course action
HMRC is bound by insolvency law as a creditor
An insolvency practitioner is not directed by HMRC

evidence.locator: Court, Scotland, insolvency and international sections
Scottish summary warrant and England/Wales powers

pipelineStages.name: Service complaint and ombudsman path
pipelineStages.trigger: The person alleges delay, poor conduct, unfair administration, failure to follow process or another service problem rather than—or in addition to—a legally appealable decision.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: HMRC handles Tier 1 and a different-person Tier 2 review; an eligible unresolved complaint can go to the Adjudicator and then PHSO through an MP.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Not every administrative wrong changes the tax merits, so a separate route addresses service, discretion, delay and maladministration.
inputs: evidence that internal stages are complete
outputs: Explanation, apology, process correction, reimbursement or recommendation within remit
pipelineStages.accountEffects: A complaint does not itself change liability, pause payment, preserve an appeal deadline or stop interest unless a separate lawful decision does so.
rightsAndSafeguards: Use statutory appeal at the same time where needed
Continue payment or seek postponement through the correct tax route
Adjudicator and PHSO have eligibility and time limits

pipelineStages.name: Value and list the property
pipelineStages.trigger: A property enters, changes within or is reviewed under the Council Tax or non-domestic rating system.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The Valuation Office maintains bands or rating lists for England and Wales; LPS combines valuation and rates functions in Northern Ireland; Scotland has its own valuation structure.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Property-based tax needs a statutory value or band before a billing authority can calculate the bill.
inputs: Property description, use and valuation evidence
outputs: Council Tax band or rateable value
valuation list entry and effective date
pipelineStages.accountEffects: The valuation feeds the later bill but is not itself a payment account or collection act.
rightsAndSafeguards: Valuation challenge and appeal routes are separate from billing complaints
Territorial procedures differ

pipelineStages.name: Calculate reliefs, issue the local bill and receive payment
pipelineStages.trigger: A listed property and liable person fall within the local tax period.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The billing authority applies the valuation, multiplier or band, discounts and reliefs, issues instalments and receives payment through its own account system.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Valuation alone does not determine the final bill; local status, relief and billing rules must be applied by the collector.
outputs: Bill, instalment schedule and local account
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Creates the local account balance. It does not appear in HMRC's Personal or Business Tax Account as one national ledger.
rightsAndSafeguards: Valuation and billing disputes use different routes
Council or LPS contact comes before assuming HMRC can fix the account

evidence.locator: How the council calculates and collects the bill
Northern Ireland rates billing and collection

pipelineStages.name: Reminder, full-year demand and liability process
pipelineStages.trigger: A local-tax instalment remains unpaid after the authority's required billing and reminder steps.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: In the published England and Wales Council Tax route the council reminds, can demand the year's balance and may seek a liability order before wages, benefits, agents or other recovery; other territories differ.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: A court-confirmed liability route gives the council access to stronger remedies after notice and non-payment.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Costs and enforcement deductions may be added under local rules; recovery remains on the local or LPS account.
rightsAndSafeguards: The court route and means inquiry apply where specified
Imprisonment in the England and Wales Council Tax guidance is exceptional and tied to culpable refusal after inquiry, not simple poverty
Territorial law must be checked

evidence.locator: Council Tax arrears and liability order

pipelineStages.name: Local-tax enforcement and insolvency branch
pipelineStages.trigger: A local authority or LPS has an enforceable unpaid local-tax debt after the territory's required notices and court or statutory gateway.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: Depending on territory and tax, the collector may use earnings or benefit deductions, certificated agents, charging or insolvency processes. England and Wales Council Tax guidance is representative only; Scotland and Northern Ireland use different law and officers.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: A final local debt still needs an enforceable recovery path, but the collector must use the territory's notices, court machinery and safeguards rather than HMRC's central-tax shortcut.
inputs: Local account and liability order or territorial equivalent
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Recovered money and permitted costs update the local account; it does not pass through the HMRC Self Assessment ledger.
rightsAndSafeguards: Territorial law and the exact tax control the remedy
A private enforcement agent needs the applicable status and power
Courts control court orders and insolvency
Complaint and liability challenges remain separate

evidence.locator: England and Wales Council Tax enforcement options

pipelineStages.name: Local valuation, billing or service challenge
pipelineStages.trigger: The person disputes value, liability, relief, billing administration or enforcement conduct.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The correct route depends on the issue: valuation check or tribunal, local billing appeal or court issue, council complaint and territorial ombudsman, or an enforcement-agent complaint.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Property value, legal liability, billing administration and agent conduct are different decisions requiring different evidence and remedies.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: A challenge changes the account only if the competent authority or court changes the underlying input or decision; not every complaint suspends payment.
rightsAndSafeguards: Use the route for the disputed decision, not a generic HMRC appeal
Observe local and territorial deadlines
Service complaint and legal appeal remain separate

pipelineStages.name: Devolved return, assessment, payment and compliance
pipelineStages.trigger: A Scottish or Welsh devolved taxable transaction, disposal or other devolved-tax event occurs.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The liable person or agent uses the territory's service to file and pay; the devolved authority may enquire, assess, require information, inspect and penalise within its own statute.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Devolved taxes need complete administration in the territory, not merely rates set locally but accounts run by HMRC.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Entries sit on the Revenue Scotland or WRA account, not a universal HMRC ledger.
rightsAndSafeguards: Use the correct devolved law and authority
Agent permission is separate from professional status
Review and appeal routes are territory-specific

pipelineStages.name: Devolved review, appeal and recovery
pipelineStages.trigger: A devolved-tax decision is challenged or a final devolved charge remains unpaid.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: The authority conducts statutory review or handles appeal under the territory's law and can use the devolved recovery and enforcement machinery once a debt is enforceable.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: A devolved authority needs its own correction and recovery path while independent tribunals and courts constrain final decisions and coercion.
pipelineStages.accountEffects: The devolved account changes only through the competent authority, review, appeal or payment; HMRC complaint and account routes do not substitute.
rightsAndSafeguards: WRA decisions may reach the Tax Chamber where statute provides
Scottish review and tribunal structures must be checked separately
Payment and interest during challenge depend on devolved law

pipelineStages.name: Risk, intelligence and fraud referral
pipelineStages.trigger: Information indicates possible serious dishonest evasion, fraudulent repayment, organised attack or another HMRC-related offence—not merely unpaid civil debt.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: Compliance, intelligence or another source refers the suspected conduct for an open-minded decision on civil fraud handling or criminal investigation.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: The most serious suspected dishonesty needs a deliberate gateway so criminal powers are not casually attached to ordinary compliance or poverty.
inputs: evidence suggesting dishonesty or organised harm
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Civil assessment, collection and penalties can continue for their proper purpose; referral alone is not guilt or a criminal charge.
rightsAndSafeguards: Decision must separate civil and criminal purposes
Suspicion is not conviction
Ordinary inability to pay is not automatically evasion

evidence.locator: Criminal investigation policy and independent charge decision
Dishonest concealment and false-return case, distinguished from simple non-payment

pipelineStages.name: Authorised criminal investigation
pipelineStages.trigger: FIS or RIS adopts suspected HMRC-related offending for criminal investigation and assigns trained authorised officers.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: Authorised officers may use jurisdiction-specific production orders, warrants, arrest, search, seizure and proceeds-of-crime powers and consult prosecutors on major cases.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Criminal evidence needs lawful compulsion, chain of custody, disclosure and suspect safeguards beyond civil enquiry powers.
inputs: warrant or order applications where required
outputs: no further action or civil referral
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Criminal investigation does not itself calculate the civil tax; civil powers may separately assess and collect when used for that purpose.
rightsAndSafeguards: Only trained authorised officers use the powers
PACE or territory-equivalent safeguards apply
Civil powers cannot be used to bypass criminal evidence rules

evidence.locator: Criminal powers, training and division from civil functions

pipelineStages.name: Independent charging decision
pipelineStages.trigger: HMRC submits an evidential case to the prosecuting authority for the relevant UK jurisdiction.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: CPS, COPFS or PPSNI applies its evidential and public-interest test, decides charges or no prosecution and controls the prosecution case.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: An independent authority checks investigator conclusions before the state brings criminal proceedings.
outputs: No charge, further work request or criminal charges
pipelineStages.accountEffects: A charging decision does not replace the separate civil tax account, though confiscation and compensation can follow conviction under their own law.
rightsAndSafeguards: Prosecutor independence
Presumption of innocence and criminal burden of proof
Defence disclosure and trial rights

pipelineStages.name: Criminal trial, sentence and asset recovery
pipelineStages.trigger: The prosecutor brings charges and the criminal court accepts jurisdiction.
pipelineStages.whatHappens: A court or jury determines guilt under the criminal standard; on conviction the court may sentence and make confiscation or other orders, subject to appeal.
pipelineStages.whyItExists: Only an independent court may impose criminal punishment after proof and defence safeguards.
inputs: asset and benefit evidence for confiscation
pipelineStages.accountEffects: Criminal orders and civil tax liabilities are legally distinct even when they arise from the same conduct.
rightsAndSafeguards: Proof beyond reasonable doubt
Legal representation, disclosure and fair-trial rights
Appeal according to the criminal jurisdiction

evidence.locator: Conviction, sentence, confiscation and appeal outcome

cases.name: HMRC v Tooth
cases.issue: Whether HMRC had a valid discovery assessment, including whether a separate staleness doctrine existed and whether the disclosed return entry was a deliberate inaccuracy.
cases.outcome: HMRC's appeal was dismissed. The Court rejected a separate staleness doctrine but held the return, read as a whole, did not contain the deliberate inaccuracy needed for HMRC's extended route.
cases.whyItMatters: It shows that a taxpayer can win even where HMRC wins a general legal point, and that assessment time limits and conduct labels must be proved precisely rather than collapsed into debt collection.

evidence.locator: Case summary, judgment details and press summary

cases.name: Bartram v HMRC
cases.issue: Whether a taxpayer has an ordinary First-tier Tribunal appeal against an HMRC determination made under Taxes Management Act 1970 section 28C after no return.
cases.outcome: The taxpayer's appeal was dismissed. No such ordinary appeal exists; the statutory remedy is a timely return that supersedes the determination, subject to the separate exceptional-relief framework.
cases.whyItMatters: A system that labels every HMRC amount 'appealable' would send users through a door the statute did not create and could let the real supersession deadline expire.

cases.name: Perrin v HMRC
cases.issue: How the reasonable-excuse test for late filing should account for the actual taxpayer's circumstances and who must first establish the penalty-triggering failure.
cases.outcome: The taxpayer's appeal was dismissed. The tribunal confirmed an objective reasonableness test applied to the actual taxpayer's circumstances and attributes.
cases.whyItMatters: It prevents both extremes: accepting any sincere belief as an excuse and judging everyone as an imaginary perfect taxpayer without their real circumstances.

cases.name: BPP Holdings v HMRC
cases.issue: Whether the tribunal could enforce its case-management rules by debarring HMRC from defending an appeal after serious and persistent procedural non-compliance.
cases.outcome: HMRC's appeal was dismissed and the tribunal's debarring response stood on the facts.
cases.whyItMatters: The revenue authority is a litigant subject to tribunal procedure; independent adjudication is not merely an internal HMRC review with optional deadlines.

evidence.locator: Case summary, judgment and press summary

cases.name: R (Haworth) v HMRC
cases.issue: The legal threshold for HMRC to issue follower notices and related accelerated-payment notices by relying on a previous judicial ruling.
cases.outcome: HMRC's appeal was dismissed and the notices remained quashed because HMRC had not met the demanding statutory threshold.
cases.whyItMatters: A powerful pay-now or penalty-backed notice needs its own conditions; policy confidence that HMRC is likely to win is not a substitute for the words Parliament enacted.

evidence.locator: Case summary, judgment and press summary

cases.name: Hurstwood Properties v Rossendale Borough Council
cases.issue: Whether business-rates avoidance schemes using special-purpose vehicle tenants defeated local-authority claims when the statutory provisions were interpreted purposively.
cases.outcome: The local authorities' appeal was unanimously allowed. The Court permitted the claims to proceed on a purposive interpretation while not treating the leases as shams or using a free-standing power to ignore companies.
cases.whyItMatters: It shows local taxes have their own collectors and litigation, and that purposive interpretation works through the statute rather than a general licence to erase corporate form.

evidence.locator: Case summary, judgment and press summary

cases.name: Totel Ltd v HMRC
cases.issue: Whether the statutory requirement to pay disputed VAT before an appeal, subject to hardship relief, unlawfully treated the taxpayer's claim less favourably.
cases.outcome: The taxpayer's appeal was dismissed and the VAT pay-first rule with hardship relief survived the challenge.
cases.whyItMatters: Payment during challenge is tax-specific: an API must not infer that direct-tax postponement and indirect-tax pay-first rules are identical.

cases.name: R v Allen
cases.issue: Criminal liability and confiscation following dishonest offshore concealment and false tax returns.
cases.outcome: The criminal appeal was dismissed; the convictions, concurrent seven-year sentences and confiscation order stood.
cases.whyItMatters: It illustrates the criminal edge for proved dishonesty and concealment, while making clear why a dataset must not label ordinary inability to pay as fraud.

evidence.locator: House of Lords judgment and order

transparencyGaps.title: No unified UK tax-adviser register
transparencyGaps.detail: ASA, HMRC registration, AML supervision, professional membership, legal authorisation, disciplinary status and client authority are held by different bodies and systems.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A client or API cannot verify 'fully licensed tax adviser' as one fact; it must resolve each permission for the intended action.

transparencyGaps.title: Agent Services Account status is not publicly verifiable
transparencyGaps.detail: HMRC explains how firms register and manage an ASA but no cited public search endpoint exposes a firm's current ASA or registration status.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Software cannot treat a firm's claim to hold an ASA as independently verified; client authority and professional status remain separate checks.

evidence.locator: Application and account guidance without public lookup

transparencyGaps.title: Software recognition exposes thin assurance metadata
transparencyGaps.detail: Public lists and recognition instructions identify suppliers, products or test processes but not tested version, security-review date, assurance evidence, uptime, incidents, ownership history or removal rationale.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Recognition can prove a tax-specific interface status, not product quality, legal correctness, security certification or continuing fitness beyond what HMRC states.

evidence.locator: Recognition instructions and limited public outputs

transparencyGaps.title: API production-access decisions are private
transparencyGaps.detail: HMRC reviews application answers privately and publishes no cited per-application production-access register or current scope record.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A third party cannot verify production access from public data, and access must never be inferred from software recognition or marketing.

transparencyGaps.title: No canonical public identity and migration crosswalk
transparencyGaps.detail: Government Gateway, One Login, software-only credentials, ASA and tax-specific enrolments coexist without one machine-readable service-by-service map.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A product can send users to the wrong sign-in or mistake identity for organisation, client or filing authority.

transparencyGaps.title: Public tax data-flow descriptions are fragmented
transparencyGaps.detail: Manuals and guidance document RTI, interest, platform and account flows separately; no cited public field-level catalogue joins all senders, systems, retention, matching and downstream uses.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Architects must cite each flow separately and cannot safely infer a universal HMRC data lake or permission from one regime to another.

transparencyGaps.title: Supplier and system dependencies are hard to reconstruct
transparencyGaps.detail: Contracts Finder awards, commercial pipeline, annual accounts, spend data and redacted schedules are separate and use scopes broader than one system component.
transparencyGaps.consequence: An award proves buyer, supplier, published scope, value and dates—not exact component ownership, spend-to-date, subcontractors, service levels or fault.

transparencyGaps.title: Valuation Office organisational names are in transition
transparencyGaps.detail: The VOA ceased as an executive agency on 1 April 2026, but official service URLs and other current pages still use the VOA name.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Data models need aliases and effective dates or they will create duplicate institutions or misstate current accountability.

evidence.locator: 1 April 2026 status change and continuing service names

transparencyGaps.title: Complaint and appeal routes do not share one case system
transparencyGaps.detail: Statutory review, tribunal appeal, HMRC complaint, Adjudicator, PHSO, professional discipline and local complaints use separate eligibility, deadlines and identifiers.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Starting a complaint can leave an appeal or payment clock running; products must present both doors and never silently redirect one into the other.

transparencyGaps.title: Published case-law coverage and appeal status are incomplete
transparencyGaps.detail: Official tribunal and court sites do not provide one complete UK-wide dataset of every decision, later appeal and territorial court result.
transparencyGaps.consequence: The case studies are selected illustrations, never a claim of exhaustive precedent or a basis for automated outcome prediction.

evidence.locator: One court's case metadata, contrasted with wider route

transparencyGaps.title: Local tax accounts and enforcement data are decentralised
transparencyGaps.detail: Councils and LPS operate different account, relief, contact and recovery systems; no common national API exposes balances, recovery state or agent assignment.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A UK-wide service can explain the framework and route users to the authority, but cannot truthfully present a live national local-tax account without separate integrations.

transparencyGaps.title: Devolved agent permissions are not publicly queryable
transparencyGaps.detail: Revenue Scotland and WRA document account and representation processes, but no cited general public API exposes current agent authority for a taxpayer.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A third party must obtain authority through the relevant service and cannot infer it from HMRC ASA or professional membership.

evidence.locator: Public service and representation information without public status lookup
Public service information without public status lookup

transparencyGaps.title: Private debt allocation and outcomes are opaque
transparencyGaps.detail: HMRC names current agencies and their permitted role but does not join the list to live case allocation, fees, commission, recovery, vulnerability or complaint metrics.
transparencyGaps.consequence: The API can say an agency may contact assigned debtors and cannot visit; it cannot say a named firm holds a person's case or is more effective or fair.

evidence.locator: Names and role limits without allocation metrics

transparencyGaps.title: Payment supplier disclosure is split
transparencyGaps.detail: Government Banking names financial partners while individual award values and operational roles can sit in separate procurement notices.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Being named proves a relationship at the stated level; it does not prove every tax payment route, contract value, service level or incident history.

transparencyGaps.title: Current and future AML supervision can be confused
transparencyGaps.detail: Current guidance still assigns supervision to HMRC and professional bodies with OPBAS oversight, while government policy plans a future FCA model after legislation and transition.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Systems must effective-date the future model and must not send today's firm to a regulator that has not yet acquired its role.

transparencyGaps.title: HMRC public guidance conflicts with current goods-enforcement fees
transparencyGaps.detail: The public non-payment page updated 17 June 2026 still shows £75, £235, £110 and a £1,500 percentage threshold; the 2026 amendment and HMRC manual show £79, £247, £116 and £1,900 for new instructions from 1 May 2026.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A legal or calculation API must prefer the effective legislation and current fee manual for the relevant instruction date and expose the stale public-page conflict rather than silently choosing.

evidence.locator: Taking possessions and old fee table
New fees, threshold and notice from 1 May 2026

transparencyGaps.title: Manual update dates do not certify every paragraph or legacy system name
transparencyGaps.detail: HMRC manuals mix current operational guidance, archive warnings and long-lived system acronyms; page-level update metadata does not show which sentence was revalidated.
transparencyGaps.consequence: Manuals must remain labelled official practice rather than primary law, and system records need transparency notes instead of pretending the public architecture is complete.

evidence.locator: Manual update metadata and named systems
Operational policy rather than statutory text

transparencyGaps.title: No public proof of one unified tax accounting ledger
transparencyGaps.detail: Published account and Debt Management manuals name tax-specific charge, record, payment, unmatched-money and debt systems and manual reallocations.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A combined online account must not be described as one authoritative database, and payment receipt must be distinguished from allocation to the intended charge.

transparencyGaps.title: The corpus does not yet expose provision-level commencement chains
transparencyGaps.detail: Acts and schedules can commence in parts, apply to different obligations on different dates and later change through regulations or commencement orders. This release links current rule families to primary material but does not assign one coarse effective date to a whole Act.
transparencyGaps.consequence: A caller must resolve the cited provision, commencement instrument, amendments and transaction or instruction date before using a rule in legal advice or a deterministic calculation.

evidence.locator: Phased dates and different adviser cohorts
